manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game.

Together with another participant, you must agree on a single set of items that will be kept. Each of you has your own view of how much each item matters to you (importance). You do not know how the other participant values the items. Additionally, you are given the effort each item demands.
You may only agree on a set if the total effort of the selected items does not exceed a shared limit:

LIMIT = 7868

Here are the individual item effort values:

Item effort = {"B21": 552, "B00": 560, "A08": 76, "A27": 950, "B94": 248, "B53": 871, "A23": 391, "C65": 143, "A89": 293, "B67": 208, "B45": 678, "C34": 737, "C12": 407, "A05": 366, "B60": 768, "C92": 859, "B69": 183, "A84": 231, "C06": 305, "B12": 727, "A16": 148, "B85": 357, "C35": 504, "B10": 549, "C15": 299, "B09": 91, "B16": 527, "C81": 848, "A58": 307, "C24": 214, "A30": 723, "A66": 475, "B75": 23, "B46": 298, "C70": 820}

Here is your personal view on the importance of each item:

Item importance values = {"B21": 138, "B00": 583, "A08": 868, "A27": 822, "B94": 783, "B53": 65, "A23": 262, "C65": 121, "A89": 508, "B67": 780, "B45": 461, "C34": 484, "C12": 668, "A05": 389, "B60": 808, "C92": 215, "B69": 97, "A84": 500, "C06": 30, "B12": 915, "A16": 856, "B85": 400, "C35": 444, "B10": 623, "C15": 781, "B09": 786, "B16": 3, "C81": 713, "A58": 457, "C24": 273, "A30": 739, "A66": 822, "B75": 235, "B46": 606, "C70": 105}

Goal:

Your goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that benefits you as much as possible (i.e., maximizes total importance to YOU), while staying within the LIMIT. You are not required to make a PROPOSAL in every message - you can simply negotiate as well. All tactics are allowed!

Interaction Protocol:

You may only use the following structured formats in a message:

PROPOSAL: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Propose keeping exactly those items.
REFUSE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Explicitly reject opponent's proposal.
ARGUMENT: {'...'}
Defend your last proposal or argue against the player's proposal.
AGREE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Accept the opponent's proposal which ends the game.
STRATEGIC REASONING: {'...'}
	Describe your strategic reasoning or anticipation explaining your choice of action. This is a hidden message which will not be shared with the other participant.

Rules:

You may only AGREE on a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
You may only REFUSE a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
Total effort of any PROPOSAL or AGREE set must be ≤ LIMIT.
Do NOT reveal your hidden importance scores.
A tag in a structured format must be followed by colon and whitespace. The argument must be a python set containing 0 or more strings.
So, it must be of the form TAG: {...}
Strictly follow the interaction protocol and DO NOT write anything beyond the given structure.
The game ends when one side gives an AGREE to a PROPOSAL made by the other player.
The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty.
Only proposals which have been logged via the PROPOSAL format structure and which haven't been refused via REFUSE are active.
You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages.
You must include the STRATEGIC REASONING format only once at the very beginning of every one of your messages and not more often. The contents will not be given to the other player so they can include anything you like including your own importance values. Here you should reason step by step to come up with you next move.

message.reasoning:
We are playing a collaborative negotiation game. The objective: we need to agree on a set of items (a subset of items) with total effort ≤ LIMIT = 7868. We have personal importance values for each item (given). The other participant also has importance values (unknown). We need to negotiate to maximize our own total importance, while staying within the limit. We can propose sets, refuse, argue, agree. The interaction protocol: we must use structured tags: PROPOSAL: {...}, REFUSE: {...}, ARGUMENT: {'...'}, AGREE: {...}, STRATEGIC REASONING: {'...'}. The STRATEGIC REASONING message is hidden from the other participant and must appear exactly once at the beginning of each of our messages. We must include ARGUMENT at least once somewhere in each message. Also, we must not reveal our hidden importance scores. We must not produce anything beyond the structured format.

We need to produce a message that includes STRATEGIC REASONING, possibly a PROPOSAL, ARGUMENT, maybe REFUSE, depending on the scenario. The conversation hasn't started yet. We need to start the negotiation. The other participant hasn't said anything yet. So we need to make a PROPOSAL (or maybe start with a strategic reasoning and argument). The rules: "The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty." So we must provide something to the other player: a PROPOSAL, ARGUMENT, etc. At the very least we need to include a PROPOSAL (or maybe we can start with an ARGUMENT?). The protocol says we may only use the structured formats. So we can produce a message that includes STRATEGIC REASONING and maybe PROPOSAL and ARGUMENT. The guidelines: "You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages." So we need to have an ARGUMENT tag. Also "You must include the STRATEGIC REASONING format only once at the very beginning of every one of your messages and not more often." So we must start with STRATEGIC REASONING.

Thus our first message should be something like:

STRATEGIC REASONING: {...}
ARGUMENT: {...}
PROPOSAL: {...}

But note the order: "The STRATEGIC REASONING format only once at the very beginning of every one of your messages and not more often." So the first line must be STRATEGIC REASONING: {...}, then we can have other tags. The order of other tags may not be strictly defined, but we might want to include ARGUMENT before PROPOSAL. The ARGUMENT can be used to defend our proposal or argue against opponent's proposal. Since there is no opponent's proposal yet, we could argue about our own proposal: e.g., we can say we think it's a fair selection, etc.

We need to produce a set of items whose total effort ≤ 7868, maximizing our importance. Since we don't know the opponent's importance values, we can try to propose a set that is high importance for us and also perhaps not too high effort, leaving room for possible opponent's items. However, the other participant may have different values. But we can try to propose a set that we think is good for us and also not too extreme.

We need to compute total effort and total importance for possible subsets. Since there are 35 items, we need to find a subset with total effort ≤ 7868 maximizing our importance. Let's compute the best solution for us (maximizing our importance) with effort constraint. Then propose that as a starting point. However, we need to be careful: the other participant may have different values and may not accept our proposal. But we can start with a high-value set for us, then we can negotiate downwards.

Alternatively, we could propose a set that is relatively balanced: includes items with high importance to us but also low effort, leaving some slack for opponent's items. Or we could propose a set that is near the limit but includes many items that we value highly.

We also need to consider that the opponent may have proposals; we may need to negotiate. But for the first message, we can propose a set that is beneficial to us.

Thus step 1: compute our importance values and effort values. Then solve a knapsack problem: maximize sum(importance_i) subject to sum(effort_i) ≤ 7868.
